description.en: Lending instead of owning — how yield, coupon, credit risk, and interest-rate risk together determine what a bond is worth.
A bond is a loan to an issuer, while a stock is ownership in a business; the rights and risks are different.

body.en: When you buy a bond, you are usually lending money to a government or company. In return, the issuer promises interest payments and repayment of principal. You are not buying ownership; you are becoming a creditor.

tip.en: Stocks ask “How much can the business grow?” Bonds ask “Will the borrower pay as promised?”

body.en: A bond has terms: face value, coupon rate, maturity date, and issuer. These define what the borrower promises. The value of the bond depends on how attractive and reliable those promises look now.

example.en: A ₸1,000,000 bond with a 10% coupon promises ₸100,000 per year until maturity, plus the principal back at the end.

body.en: A shareholder can benefit greatly if a company grows, but also bears ownership risk. A bondholder's upside is usually capped at promised payments. The trade-off is priority: creditors normally stand ahead of shareholders if the issuer fails.

tip.en: Lower upside does not mean no risk; it means a different type of risk.

blocks.text: Imagine a company raises money in two ways: it sells shares and issues bonds. Both investors fund the same business, but their claims are not the same.
The same company can be a reasonable bond investment and a risky stock investment, or the reverse, depending on price and risk.

body.en: Classify each statement as describing bonds, stocks, or both. The goal is to separate lending logic from ownership logic.

en.instruction: Drag each statement to the best category.

items.text: The issuer promises scheduled interest payments
The investor owns a fraction of the business
The investor can lose money if the business weakens
The contract has a date when principal should be repaid

en.explanation: Bonds and stocks can both lose value, but they give investors different claims: lending promises versus ownership participation.

body.en: A bond is best understood as a loan contract. The investor cares about promised cash flows, repayment, and the issuer's ability to keep the promise. That makes bond analysis less about unlimited growth and more about reliability, price, and risk.

tip.en: Before buying a bond, identify the borrower, the promise, and what could stop repayment.

question_text.en: A company grows rapidly after you buy its bond. What is the most realistic expectation?

answer_text.en: You usually receive the promised bond payments, but do not automatically share all growth like an owner
Your bond return becomes unlimited like a stock
You become a shareholder because the business improved
The company must double your coupon rate

question_text.en: Why might a bondholder focus more on downside protection than huge upside?

answer_text.en: The bondholder's main claim is scheduled payment and principal repayment
Bondholders legally own all future profits
Bonds have no risk if bought at issue
Bond prices cannot change after purchase

question_text.en: A firm has both shareholders and bondholders. If the firm fails, why are bondholders usually treated differently?

answer_text.en: They are creditors with contractual claims, often ahead of owners
They are always guaranteed by the stock exchange
They automatically become government lenders
They receive all profits before taxes every year

description.en: Coupon is the promised payment rate, while yield is the return implied by the bond's current price and cash flows.

title.en: Coupon is not the whole return

body.en: A bond's coupon tells you the scheduled interest payment. Yield tells you what return the current price implies if payments happen as expected. The two are equal only in special cases.

title.en: Coupon is based on face value

body.en: The coupon rate is applied to the bond's face value, not always to the price you pay. A ₸1,000,000 bond with an 8% coupon pays ₸80,000 per year. That payment does not change just because the market price changes.

tip.en: Coupon answers “How much cash is promised?”

body.en: If you buy a bond below face value, the same coupon becomes more attractive and you may also gain when principal is repaid. If you buy above face value, the coupon may look high but part of your price can be lost at maturity. Yield connects all cash flows to the price paid.

tip.en: Yield answers “What return does this price imply?”

blocks.text: Three investors buy the same ₸1,000,000 face-value bond with an 8% coupon. Each receives ₸80,000 per year, but they pay different prices.

rows: Higher return: coupon plus price gain
Lower return: coupon partly offset by price loss

blocks.text: The promised coupon is the same. The investor's return changes because the price paid changes.

body.en: Choose whether each statement is about the coupon payment, the yield, or the price effect behind yield.

en.instruction: Select the concept that best matches each statement.

scenarios.text: A bond with ₸1,000,000 face value and 9% coupon pays ₸90,000 per year.
scenarios.explanation: The statement calculates the scheduled cash payment from face value.

scenarios.text: A bond bought below face value can return more than its coupon rate if it is repaid at face value.
scenarios.explanation: The investor may benefit from the gap between purchase price and repayment value.

scenarios.text: An investor compares two bonds by the return implied by today's market price and all future payments.
scenarios.explanation: Yield combines coupon, price, repayment, and time.

body.en: Coupon tells you the promised cash payment; yield tells you the return implied by today's price. A high coupon is not automatically a high return if the bond is expensive. A low coupon can still have an attractive yield if the price is low enough.

tip.en: For decisions, yield is usually more useful than coupon alone.

question_text.en: A ₸1,000,000 face-value bond has a 10% coupon but trades for ₸1,150,000. What should you understand?

answer_text.en: The coupon is ₸100,000 per year, but the yield may be below 10% because the purchase price is high
The investor will receive ₸115,000 per year because price changed
The bond cannot trade above face value
The yield must equal the coupon in every case

question_text.en: Why can a bond bought below face value have a yield higher than its coupon rate?

answer_text.en: The investor receives coupon payments and may gain when principal is repaid at face value
The issuer must increase the coupon after the sale
The broker pays extra interest on discounted bonds
A lower price removes all credit risk

question_text.en: Which number best helps compare two bonds with different coupons and market prices?

answer_text.en: Yield, because it connects price with expected cash flows
Coupon only, because price is irrelevant
Face value only, because all bonds repay the same amount
The number of pages in the bond prospectus

description.en: Credit risk is the chance that the issuer cannot pay interest or principal as promised.

body.en: A bond is built on a promise, but promises are only as strong as the issuer's ability to pay. Credit risk is the risk that interest or principal is delayed, reduced, or not paid. Higher promised yield often compensates for this possibility.

A borrower with unstable cash flow must offer a higher yield to attract lenders. The higher yield is not a gift; it is compensation for taking more risk. If the issuer's condition worsens, the bond price can fall before any missed payment occurs.

tip.en: Extra yield is payment for extra uncertainty.

title.en: Default is not the only loss

body.en: Investors can lose money even without formal default. If the market believes default risk has increased, buyers demand a higher yield, which pushes the existing bond's price down. Credit risk is priced continuously.

blocks.text: Two five-year bonds both have ₸1,000,000 face value. One issuer is financially stable; the other has falling revenue and heavy debt.
The highest yield may be the most dangerous. The return is attractive only if the issuer actually pays.

body.en: Identify the main credit-risk signal in each situation.

scenarios.text: A company has stable cash flow and low debt compared with earnings.
scenarios.explanation: Stable cash generation and manageable debt support payment ability.

scenarios.text: A bond's yield jumps after the issuer reports a sharp revenue decline.
scenarios.explanation: Investors may now doubt the issuer's ability to pay.

scenarios.text: A weak issuer offers much more yield than a strong issuer with the same maturity.
scenarios.explanation: The higher yield compensates investors for accepting weaker repayment quality.

body.en: Credit risk turns bond investing into borrower analysis. The promised cash flow is only valuable if the issuer can deliver it. Before chasing a high yield, ask why the yield is high and what would happen if the issuer's cash flow weakens.

tip.en: A bond yield is attractive only after adjusting for the chance of not receiving it.

question_text.en: A corporate bond yields 22% while similar government bonds yield 9%. What is the most careful interpretation?

answer_text.en: The market may be demanding compensation for much higher credit risk
The corporate bond is automatically better because the yield is higher
The corporate bond cannot default if it pays a coupon
Government bonds and corporate bonds always have equal risk

question_text.en: An issuer has not missed any payments, but its bond price falls after its debt rises sharply. Why?

answer_text.en: Investors may price in a higher probability of future payment problems
Bond prices can only fall after default
Debt increases always make bonds safer
Coupons are canceled whenever debt rises

question_text.en: Which question best targets credit risk?

answer_text.en: Can this issuer generate enough cash to pay interest and principal on time?
Did the bond price rise yesterday?
Is the coupon number easy to remember?
Does the bond have a short name?

description.en: Bond prices move when market interest rates change, especially for longer-term fixed-rate bonds.

body.en: A fixed-rate bond promises payments set in the past. When market rates change, those old payments become more or less attractive. That is why bond prices move even when the issuer is still healthy.

If new bonds offer higher yields, older bonds with lower coupons must fall in price to compete. If new yields fall, older higher-coupon bonds become more valuable. Bond prices and market yields usually move in opposite directions.

tip.en: For existing fixed-rate bonds: rates up usually means price down.

body.en: Longer-term bonds are usually more sensitive to rate changes because their fixed payments stretch further into the future. A short bond returns principal sooner, letting the investor reinvest at new rates. Time makes old promises more exposed to new conditions.

title.en: Why the old bond must discount

blocks.text: You hold a five-year bond paying 8%. New similar bonds now yield 12%. A buyer will not pay full price for your lower-yielding bond.
The issuer did not necessarily become riskier. The price adjusted because the market opportunity changed.

body.en: Choose what usually happens to an existing fixed-rate bond in each rate scenario.

scenarios.text: Market yields rise from 9% to 13% after you bought a fixed-rate bond.
scenarios.explanation: New bonds are more attractive, so the old bond price must adjust lower.

scenarios.text: Market yields fall, and your older bond has a higher coupon than new bonds.
scenarios.explanation: The old bond's cash flows are now more attractive.

scenarios.text: A bond matures in three months rather than ten years.
scenarios.explanation: Shorter time to repayment usually reduces sensitivity to rate changes.

body.en: Interest-rate risk is separate from credit risk. A bond can lose market value because rates changed, even if the issuer remains reliable. The longer the fixed cash flows last, the more the price usually reacts to changing yields.

tip.en: Do not confuse a rate-driven price fall with proof of default risk.

question_text.en: You bought a fixed-rate bond yielding 8%. New similar bonds now yield 12%. What is likely to happen to your bond's market price?

answer_text.en: It will likely fall so its return becomes more competitive with new bonds
It will rise because higher market rates help all existing bonds
It must stay at face value until maturity
Its coupon automatically increases to 12%

question_text.en: Why is a 15-year fixed-rate bond usually more sensitive to interest-rate changes than a 6-month bond?

answer_text.en: Its fixed payments are locked in for much longer
Long bonds have no coupons
Short bonds always default first
Maturity length has no relation to price sensitivity

question_text.en: A reliable government bond falls in price after rates rise. What is the best explanation?

answer_text.en: The price may be adjusting to higher market yields, not necessarily default fear
The government has definitely defaulted
Reliable bonds cannot change price
The coupon payment has disappeared

description.en: Government, corporate, inflation-linked, floating-rate, and short-term bonds solve different investor problems.

title.en: Not all bonds solve the same problem

body.en: People often say “bonds” as if they are one product. In reality, bond types differ by issuer, maturity, coupon structure, currency, and inflation protection. The right type depends on which risk you are trying to manage.

Government bonds usually depend on the state's ability and willingness to pay. Corporate bonds depend on a company's cash flow and balance sheet. A higher corporate yield may reflect extra credit risk, not simply a better deal.

tip.en: The first bond type question is: who is the borrower?

title.en: Structure defines rate and inflation exposure

body.en: Fixed-rate bonds give predictable coupons but can lose price when rates rise. Floating-rate bonds adjust with rates, reducing some rate risk. Inflation-linked bonds try to protect purchasing power, but their prices can still move.

title.en: Match the bond to the job

blocks.text: An investor has ₸2,000,000 and wants part of it in bonds. Different bond choices solve different problems.

rows: May behave differently from fixed bonds

blocks.text: Choosing a bond starts with the job: safety, income, inflation protection, rate flexibility, or timing.

body.en: Pick the bond type that best matches each investor's main problem.

en.instruction: Select the most suitable bond type for the stated need.

scenarios.text: An investor wants lower default risk and accepts a lower yield.
scenarios.explanation: Government bonds often focus on repayment reliability rather than maximum income.

scenarios.text: An investor accepts extra issuer risk to seek higher income.
scenarios.explanation: Corporate bonds may offer more yield to compensate for credit risk.

scenarios.text: An investor worries that market rates may rise soon.
scenarios.explanation: Shorter maturity or floating coupons can reduce exposure to rising rates.

scenarios.text: An investor wants bond payments linked more directly to purchasing power.
scenarios.explanation: Inflation-linked structures are designed to address inflation risk.

body.en: Bond types are tools for different risks. Government versus corporate changes credit exposure; short versus long changes rate sensitivity; fixed, floating, and inflation-linked structures change how cash flows respond to the economy. Choose the type by naming the risk you want to manage.

tip.en: Do not buy a bond category because it sounds safe; match it to the specific job.

question_text.en: An investor wants the lowest reasonable default risk and accepts a lower yield. Which bond type is the most natural starting point?

answer_text.en: Government bonds from a reliable issuer
The highest-yield distressed corporate bond
A long bond from a company with falling revenue
Any bond with the largest coupon number

question_text.en: Why might an investor choose a floating-rate bond when rates may rise?

answer_text.en: Its payments can adjust with rates, reducing some fixed-rate price pressure
It guarantees no credit risk
It always pays more than every corporate bond
It cannot fall in price under any condition

question_text.en: A person needs money for a planned expense in one year. Which bond feature matters most?

answer_text.en: A maturity or low-risk structure that matches the timing of the goal
The longest possible maturity for maximum rate sensitivity
The highest coupon regardless of issuer quality
A bond type chosen only because friends bought it

question_text.en: A friend says bonds are the same as stocks because both can be bought through a broker. What is the key correction?

answer_text.en: A bond is usually a lending claim, while a stock is an ownership claim
Bonds and stocks always pay identical returns
Stocks have maturity dates and bonds never do
Bonds give unlimited ownership upside

question_text.en: A ₸1,000,000 face-value bond with a 9% coupon trades for ₸900,000. Why can yield be above 9%?

answer_text.en: The investor receives the coupon and may gain if principal is repaid at face value
The coupon automatically rises because the price is lower
The issuer must pay interest on the market price only
Buying below face value removes all risk

question_text.en: A bond offers 24% yield while similar safer issuers offer 10%. What should you investigate first?

answer_text.en: Whether the higher yield reflects serious credit risk
Whether the bond name is easy to remember
Whether all high-yield bonds are risk-free bargains
Whether the coupon is paid in round numbers

question_text.en: Market rates rise after you buy a long-term fixed-rate bond. What is the usual pressure on its market price?

answer_text.en: Downward pressure, because new bonds offer more attractive yields
Upward pressure, because all rates are higher
No pressure, because bond prices never move
Automatic conversion into a floating-rate bond

question_text.en: Which bond choice best matches someone worried about rising rates over the next year?

answer_text.en: A short-term or floating-rate bond
The longest fixed-rate bond available
Only the bond with the highest coupon
A corporate bond chosen without checking issuer risk
